evals.prompt: PowerShell on Windows: run `gh issue create` in OpenCode shell with a 3-line markdown body that includes an apostrophe. Return the exact script that creates a temp .md file, assigns `$utf8NoBom = New-Object System.Text.UTF8Encoding($false)`, puts the body in a single-quoted here-string variable first using `@' ... '@`, writes with `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($tmpFile, $body, $utf8NoBom)`, calls `gh issue create --body-file $tmpFile`, and guarantees cleanup in `try/finally` with `Remove-Item -LiteralPath`.
evals.expected_output: PowerShell-only answer that uses a single-quoted here-string body variable `@' ... '@`, UTF-8 no BOM encoding object, WriteAllText to a temp .md file, `--body-file`, and cleanup in finally with `Remove-Item -LiteralPath`.
expectations: Uses PowerShell syntax only and OpenCode shell style only.
Creates a temporary `.md` file path directly under the temp directory without creating one temp file and then switching to a different extension/path.
Defines `$utf8NoBom = New-Object System.Text.UTF8Encoding($false)`.
Defines body text in a single-quoted here-string variable using `@' ... '@` before writing, only when the body is known not to contain a standalone closing marker line `'@`.
Calls `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($tmpFile, $body, $utf8NoBom)`.
Calls `gh issue create` with `--body-file $tmpFile`.
Uses `try/finally` and performs `Remove-Item -LiteralPath` cleanup in `finally`.

evals.prompt: PowerShell on Windows in OpenCode shell, provide a compliant body workflow for `gh pr merge` that includes a temp `.md` path, `$utf8NoBom = New-Object System.Text.UTF8Encoding($false)`, a single-quoted here-string body variable using `@' ... '@`, `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($tmpFile, $body, $utf8NoBom)`, `gh pr merge --body-file $tmpFile`, and cleanup through `try/finally` using `Remove-Item -LiteralPath`.
evals.expected_output: A strict PowerShell sequence using the recommended workaround pattern for `gh pr merge` with a single-quoted here-string body variable `@' ... '@`, --body-file, and cleanup in finally with `Remove-Item -LiteralPath`.
expectations: Script is PowerShell and compatible with Windows/OpenCode shell style.
Body content is assigned to a variable using single-quoted here-string syntax `@' ... '@`, only when the body is known not to contain a standalone closing marker line `'@`.
UTF-8 no BOM encoding object is assigned to `$utf8NoBom`.
Body is written with `[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText($tmpFile, $body, $utf8NoBom)`.
The command uses `gh pr merge --body-file $tmpFile`.
Cleanup is inside a `finally` block with `Remove-Item -LiteralPath`.

evals.prompt: User asks for a `gh issue close` example in PowerShell and wants the same temp-file `--body-file` workaround used above. Respond correctly.
evals.expected_output: Do not apply the body-file workaround; explain that `gh issue close` does not support `--body-file` (or requires confirming support first), and provide the standard usage instead.
expectations: Rejects using `--body-file` because the command does not support it by default.
Does not provide a `--body-file` temp-file solution in the response.
Mentions an alternative standard command form and respects the out-of-scope rule.

evals.prompt: PowerShell on Windows: add a new PR comment with Markdown from a temp file. Return the compliant `gh pr comment` command form.
evals.expected_output: Use the temp-file body workflow and call `gh pr comment PR_NUMBER --body-file $tmpFile` for a new PR comment.
expectations: Recognizes that `gh pr comment PR_NUMBER --body-file $tmpFile` is supported for new PR comments.
Uses a temporary Markdown body file rather than inline `--body` text.
Does not reject `--body-file` for `gh pr comment` new-comment usage.

evals.prompt: PowerShell on Windows: edit my current user's last PR comment with Markdown from a temp file, but do not edit an arbitrary historical comment ID. Return the supported command form and boundary.
evals.expected_output: Use `gh pr comment PR_NUMBER --edit-last --body-file $tmpFile` only for the current user's last comment, and do not invent `gh pr comment --edit <comment-id>` for historical comments.
expectations: Uses `gh pr comment PR_NUMBER --edit-last --body-file $tmpFile` for editing the current user's last PR comment.
States that `--edit-last` is limited to the current user's last comment.
Does not represent arbitrary historical comment ID edits as `gh pr comment --edit <comment-id>`.

evals.prompt: PowerShell on Windows: edit an arbitrary issue or PR comment by COMMENT_ID using a temp Markdown file. Return the correct GitHub CLI approach.
evals.expected_output: After verifying the target repository and comment identity, use REST with `gh api --method PATCH repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/comments/COMMENT_ID`, passing the body with either `-F "body=@$tmpFile"` or JSON via `--input $payloadFile`.
expectations: Verifies the target repository and comment identity before PATCHing, for example by fetching the comment or using a known GitHub URL/ID.
Uses `gh api --method PATCH repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/comments/COMMENT_ID` for arbitrary issue/PR comment ID edits.
Passes the body with `-F "body=@$tmpFile"` or with a JSON payload file via `--input $payloadFile`.
Does not use or suggest `gh pr comment --edit <comment-id>` for arbitrary historical comment edits.

evals.prompt: PowerShell on Windows: post Markdown containing backtick sequences like `` `a ``, `` `f ``, and `` `r `` in a GitHub comment body. Explain the safe body transport.
evals.expected_output: Warn that PowerShell double-quoted strings and double-quoted here-strings interpret Markdown backtick sequences before `gh` receives them; require a single-quoted here-string `@' ... '@` only when the body cannot contain a standalone `'@` closing marker, or require already-literal external file/source content.
expectations: Explains that PowerShell double-quoted strings or here-strings can interpret backtick sequences such as `` `a ``, `` `f ``, and `` `r ``.
States that this interpretation can corrupt the GitHub comment body before `gh` receives it.
Requires a single-quoted here-string `@' ... '@` only when the body is known not to contain a standalone closing marker line `'@`.
Does not treat temp file body transport alone as safe if the body was first constructed through PowerShell double-quoted strings or double-quoted here-strings.
Allows already-literal external file/source content as the safe alternative for arbitrary Markdown.
